cards.back: When execution flows from one case into the next case because there is no break statement, executing multiple case blocks sequentially.

cards.question: What will this code print if x = 1?
cards.explanation: Case 1 has no break, so execution falls through to case 2. Both "a" and "b" are printed. This is fallthrough behavior.

cards.front: Is fallthrough in switch statements usually intentional or accidental?
cards.back: Usually accidental and a bug. Most of the time, programmers forget to add break statements. Intentional fallthrough is rare.

cards.text: To indicate intentional fallthrough and silence compiler warnings, use the {{c1::[[fallthrough]]}} attribute before the next case label.

cards.question: How do you properly document intentional fallthrough?
cards.explanation: The [[fallthrough]] attribute tells the compiler and readers that the fallthrough is intentional, suppressing warnings.

cards.front: Should you include a break statement after the last case in a switch?
cards.back: It's not necessary but recommended for consistency and to prevent bugs if you later add more cases.

cards.front: Do case statements create their own scope?
cards.back: No, all statements inside a switch are in the same scope. You must use explicit braces { } to create a new scope.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Variable y is declared in case 1 without a block scope. Case 2 can see y but it may not be initialized.
cards.explanation: All cases share the same scope. Variable y is visible in case 2, but if case 2 executes first, y is uninitialized. Use braces to create proper scopes.

cards.concept: Proper variable declaration in switch cases
cards.explanation:
To declare variables in a switch case:
  • Wrap the case in braces { } to create a block scope
  • This prevents the variable from being visible to other cases
  • It ensures the variable is only in scope where it's initialized
  • Without braces, the variable is visible to all subsequent cases, causing potential initialization issues
examples:
// Wrong
case 1:
    int x = 5; // visible in other cases!
    break;

cards.text: When declaring a variable in a switch case, you must create a {{c1::block}} using {{c2::curly braces}} to properly scope the variable.

cards.front: What are the two main issues with switch fallthrough?
cards.back:
1) It's usually unintentional and causes bugs
2) It makes code harder to understand and maintain

cards.explanation:
Follow these practices for clean switch statements:
  • Always use break unless fallthrough is intentional
  • Document intentional fallthrough with [[fallthrough]]
  • Place default case last
  • Use braces { } when declaring variables in cases
  • Consider adding break even after the last case
  • Enable compiler warnings about fallthrough
examples:
case 2:
{ // Good: braces for variables
    int x = 5;
    break;
}
